title: Generation of magnetic metal-organic frameworks
authors: Alexander C. Tyner, Avinash Pathapati, Alexander V. Balatsky
abstract: The potential to utilize metal-organic frameworks as a replacement for rare earth materials as well as in technological applications has prompted increased interested in this material class. The simulation of organic materials, including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), represents a computational challenge due to an increased average number of atoms in the unit cell. Compounding this challenge, modern materials databases are generally limited to inorganic structures due to their utility in modern technologies such as batteries and integrated circuits. Machine-learning tools appear ideally suited to study these systems. However, organic materials are generally underrepresented in the training sets of foundational models. In this work we leverage the the Organic Materials Database (OMDB) to create a training dataset comprised of more than 15,000 single-point first-principles computations for finetuning machine learned interatomic potentials. Specifically, we fine tune CHGNet and implement a site substitution workflow to identify novel, highly magnetic, MOFs from structural prototypes within the QMOF database.
summary: This paper addresses the difficulty of simulating large-scale organic materials like MOFs by fine-tuning a machine-learning interatomic potential (CHGNet) with a new dataset of 15,000+ computations. The researchers developed a workflow to discover new, highly magnetic metal-organic frameworks by substituting sites in known structural prototypes. This approach provides a scalable way to explore the vast chemical space of magnetic organic materials.
main_problem: Simulating metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) is computationally challenging due to large unit cells and the underrepresentation of organic materials in existing machine-learning training sets.
main_result: The authors successfully fine-tuned the CHGNet interatomic potential using a new dataset of 15,000+ first-principles computations from the OMDB and implemented a workflow to identify novel magnetic MOFs.
method: Fine-tuning the CHGNet machine-learned interatomic potential using a dataset derived from the Organic Materials Database (OMDB) and applying a site substitution workflow on QMOF structural prototypes.
model_or_system: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) characterized by large unit cells containing both organic and inorganic components, specifically targeting the generation of highly magnetic structures.
key_observables: Magnetic properties of the identified MOFs
main_assumptions: The effectiveness of fine-tuning CHGNet relies on the representativeness of the 15,000 single-point first-principles computations from the OMDB.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational challenge of MOFs, describes the creation of a new training dataset from OMDB, details the fine-tuning of the CHGNet model, and presents a workflow for identifying new magnetic MOFs.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
